text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
Next-Generation Sequencing — Solid Tumor Panel

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Patient Name:         Raymond Kowalski
Date of Birth:        [on file]
MRN:                  [on file]
Ordering Physician:   [Treating Oncologist], MD — Medical Oncology
Diagnosis:            Adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon, Stage IV
                      (cT4aN2bM1a; synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases)
Specimen ID:          SP-[XXXXXX]-CRC-01
Specimen Type:        Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue
Specimen Source:      Colonoscopic biopsy — sigmoid colon primary mass
Date of Procedure:    [on file]
Tumor Cellularity:    Estimated 55% (pathologist-reviewed H&E prior to DNA extraction)
Sequencing Platform:  Comprehensive 500-gene targeted panel (DNA-seq and RNA-seq
                      fusion analysis); paired germline comparator (peripheral blood
                      leukocytes) for somatic/germline variant classification
Mean Coverage:        500x (tumor); 200x (germline)
Analysis Version:     Pipeline v4.2.1
Report Date:          [on file]

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUMMARY OF CLINICALLY RELEVANT FINDINGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC SOMATIC ALTERATIONS DETECTED:

  1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A), Exon 2 — PATHOGENIC / ONCOGENIC DRIVER
     Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42.3%
     Classification: Activating point mutation; RAS GTPase domain impairment
     Clinical relevance: Precludes use of anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies
     (cetux, pani). Consistent with left-sided colorectal adenocarcinoma
     biology. KRAS G12D accounts for approximately 12% of all KRAS-mutant CRC cases.
     Investigational KRAS G12D-selective covalent inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133-class
     compounds, adagrasib, sotorasib) are under active clinical investigation in CRC;
     response rates in colorectal histology are lower than in non-small cell lung
     cancer, likely due to feedback reactivation via EGFR and concurrent RAS pathway
     co-alterations.

  2. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T), Exon 7 — PATHOGENIC
     VAF: 39.1%
     Classification: Gain-of-function / dominant-negative missense mutation at
     hotspot codon 248 (one of the most frequently mutated codons in human cancer).
     p.R248W abrogates wild-type p53 transactivation and confers oncogenic
     gain-of-function properties including enhanced invasion and chemotherapy
     resistance. Present in approximately 20-25% of colorectal carcinomas.
     Clinical relevance: No direct therapeutic implication; prognostic marker
     of aggressive disease biology.

  3. APC p.Q1367* (c.4099C>T), Exon 15 — PATHOGENIC
     VAF: 44.7%
     Classification: Nonsense (truncating) mutation resulting in loss of the
     C-terminal armadillo/SAMP repeat domains critical for beta-catenin binding
     and destruction complex function. APC truncation is a canonical early event
     in conventional adenoma-to-carcinoma progression and is present in
     approximately 70-80% of sporadic colorectal cancers. Consistent with
     chromosomal instability (CIN) pathway of colorectal carcinogenesis.
     Clinical relevance: Constitutive Wnt/beta-catenin pathway activation;
     no direct actionable implication at this time.

  4. SMAD4 p.R361H (c.1082G>A), Exon 9 — PATHOGENIC
     VAF: 18.2%
     Classification: Missense mutation disrupting the MH2 domain of SMAD4
     (DPC4), a central mediator of TGF-beta/BMP tumor-suppressive signaling.
     SMAD4 loss or inactivation occurs in approximately 25-30% of colorectal
     carcinomas and is enriched in metastatic disease. Multiple retrospective
     analyses have associated SMAD4 loss with synchronous hepatic metastases,
     shorter progression-free survival, and reduced benefit from anti-VEGF
     therapy, though the latter remains investigational and does not constitute
     a clinical contraindication to bev use.
     Clinical relevance: Adverse prognostic biomarker; potential marker of
     limited anti-VEGF benefit. Does not preclude bev-based therapy.
     Germline SMAD4 pathogenic variants are associated with juvenile polyposis
     syndrome; no germline variant detected in this patient (somatic only).

  5. PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A), Exon 9 — PATHOGENIC
     VAF: 11.4%
     Classification: Activating missense mutation in the helical domain of
     PI3K catalytic subunit alpha. PIK3CA exon 9 (E545K) and exon 20 (H1047R)
     hotspot mutations are the two most common PIK3CA alterations in CRC,
     occurring in approximately 15-20% of cases. This mutation activates
     PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling independent of upstream RTK input.
     Clinical relevance: Potentially actionable. PI3K pathway inhibitors
     (alpelisib, copanlisib, taselisib) are under investigation in CRC.
     Retrospective data suggest PIK3CA-mutant CRC patients may derive benefit
     from aspirin (COX-2/PIK3CA interaction). Clinical trial enrollment for
     PI3K pathway-targeted agents should be considered. Note: in the setting
     of concurrent KRAS mutation, PI3K pathway inhibition alone is unlikely
     to be sufficient.

  6. FBXW7 p.R465C (c.1393C>T), Exon 9 — LIKELY PATHOGENIC
     VAF: 9.8%
     Classification: Missense mutation at a recurrently mutated residue of
     FBXW7 (F-box and WD repeat domain-containing 7), an E3 ubiquitin ligase
     component that targets oncoproteins including cyclin E, c-MYC, c-JUN,
     NOTCH, and mTOR for proteasomal degradation. FBXW7 loss-of-function
     leads to stabilization of multiple oncogenic substrates. FBXW7 is mutated
     in approximately 10% of CRCs.
     Clinical relevance: Candidate predictive biomarker for CDK4/6 inhibitor
     sensitivity (via cyclin E stabilization); no standard therapeutic
     implication currently.

  7. ARID1A p.Q2197* (c.6589C>T), Exon 20 — LIKELY PATHOGENIC
     VAF: 7.3%
     Classification: Nonsense (truncating) mutation in AT-rich interactive
     domain-containing protein 1A (ARID1A/BAF250a), a subunit of the SWI/SNF
     chromatin remodeling complex. ARID1A is a tumor suppressor mutated in
     approximately 8-12% of colorectal carcinomas. ARID1A loss has been
     associated with defective SWI/SNF complex assembly and aberrant
     transcriptional regulation. Notably, ARID1A-deficient tumors show
     synthetic lethality with ARID1B, EZH2 inhibition, and PARP inhibition
     in preclinical models; clinical translation is ongoing.
     Clinical relevance: Candidate predictive biomarker; no standard
     therapeutic implication currently.

  8. TCF7L2 p.E225* (c.673G>T), Exon 7 — VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
     VAF: 8.1%
     Classification: Nonsense mutation in TCF7L2 (Transcription Factor 7-Like 2),
     a Wnt pathway transcription factor. While loss-of-function TCF7L2 alterations
     have been described in CRC, this specific variant has not been functionally
     characterized and cannot be classified as pathogenic at this time. Classified
     as VUS pending further evidence.
     Clinical relevance: None established.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  MYC AMPLIFICATION (8q24.21)
     Copy number: 9.4 (estimated; relative to ploidy-adjusted baseline)
     Classification: Pathogenic amplification
     Clinical relevance: MYC amplification is present in 10-15% of CRCs and
     is associated with aggressive tumor behavior, enhanced proliferative
     signaling, and resistance to chemotherapy via upregulation of anti-apoptotic
     programs. No direct therapeutic target; indirect relevance to CDK7, BRD4,
     and aurora kinase inhibitor trials targeting MYC-amplified tumors.

  CCND1 (Cyclin D1) GAIN (11q13.3)
     Copy number: 4.1
     Classification: Low-level gain
     Clinical relevance: Cyclin D1 gain promotes CDK4/6-dependent cell cycle
     progression. In the context of concurrent FBXW7 alteration (which impairs
     cyclin E degradation), this tumor may have enhanced CDK4/6-cyclin D and
     CDK2-cyclin E dependencies. Potentially relevant to CDK4/6 inhibitor trials.

  CDKN2A HOMOZYGOUS DELETION (9p21.3)
     Copy number: 0 (homozygous deletion confirmed by SNP array-like algorithm;
     encompasses both p16/INK4a and p14/ARF loci)
     Classification: Pathogenic tumor suppressor loss
     Clinical relevance: Loss of both p16 (CDK4/6 inhibitor) and p14/ARF
     (p53 stabilizer) accelerates cell cycle progression and abrogates the
     ARF-p53 tumor suppressive axis (note: TP53 is independently mutated in this
     tumor, compounding this effect). CDKN2A homozygous deletion is present in
     approximately 5-10% of CRCs and is associated with poor prognosis.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GENE FUSIONS / STRUCTURAL REARRANGEMENTS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  NTRK1, NTRK2, NTRK3:   NO FUSION DETECTED
  RET:                    NO FUSION DETECTED
  ALK:                    NO FUSION DETECTED
  ROS1:                   NO FUSION DETECTED
  FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3:   NO FUSION DETECTED
  RSPO2, RSPO3:           NO FUSION DETECTED
  No actionable gene fusions identified.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BIOMAREKR PANEL — NEGATIVE / WILD-TYPE FINDINGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  KRAS Exons 3 & 4 (codons 59, 61, 117, 146):   WILD-TYPE
  NRAS Exons 2, 3, 4:                            WILD-TYPE
  BRAF Exon 11 (p.G469):                         WILD-TYPE
  BRAF Exon 15 (p.V600E):                        WILD-TYPE
  HER2 (ERBB2):                                  NOT AMPLIFIED (copy number 2.1;
                                                 no activating mutation detected)
  HER3 (ERBB3):                                  WILD-TYPE
  MET:                                           WILD-TYPE (no amplification,
                                                 no exon 14 skip mutation)
  PTEN:                                          NO PATHOGENIC MUTATION DETECTED
                                                 (note: copy number neutral)
  RAS pathway (overall):                         KRAS G12D mutant; all other
                                                 RAS/RAF components wild-type

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) AND MISMATCH REPAIR (MMR)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  MSI Status (NGS-based fragment analysis, 7 loci):   MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  MMR Protein Status (IHC concordance):               PROFICIENT MMR
     MLH1:  Retained expression
     MSH2:  Retained expression
     MSH6:  Retained expression
     PMS2:  Retained expression
  Interpretation: MSS/pMMR status indicates no deficiency in mismatch repair
  machinery. Standard-of-care immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy
  (Keytruda, Opdivo) as single agents have minimal activity in MSS
  CRC and are not indicated outside of a clinical trial in this setting.
  Lynch syndrome germline testing: No germline pathogenic variants identified
  in MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2 on paired blood analysis. MLH1 promoter
  methylation: not detected. Findings are consistent with sporadic colorectal
  carcinoma, not Lynch syndrome.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  TMB: 6.2 mutations/megabase (LOW)
  Interpretation: TMB below the high threshold (≥10 mut/Mb, FDA-approved
  pembro indication). Low TMB is consistent with chromosomal instability
  (CIN) pathway CRC, which typically shows lower somatic mutation rates than
  hypermutated/MSI-H CRC. Not predictive of benefit from immune checkpoint
  inhibitors in this context.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GERMLINE FINDINGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  No germline pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants identified in the
  following hereditary colorectal cancer genes: APC (familial adenomatous
  polyposis), MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM (Lynch syndrome), MUTYH,
  STK11 (Peutz-Jeghers), SMAD4 (juvenile polyposis), PTEN (PTEN hamartoma
  tumor syndrome), BRCA1, BRCA2. Findings are consistent with sporadic
  (non-hereditary) colorectal carcinoma. Genetic counseling referral not
  urgently indicated based on molecular findings, though referral may be
  considered per institutional guidelines given patient age <60 at diagnosis.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTEGRATED GENOMIC INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This tumor demonstrates a genomic profile consistent with the chromosomal
instability (CIN) subtype of sporadic colorectal adenocarcinoma, with a
molecular architecture typical of left-sided, RAS-mutant metastatic CRC.
The co-occurring mutations in KRAS (G12D), TP53 (R248W), and APC (Q1367*)
represent the canonical "Vogelstein pathway" of colorectal carcinogenesis
(APC loss → RAS activation → TP53 inactivation). The additional somatic
alterations in SMAD4, PIK3CA, FBXW7, and ARID1A represent secondary
clonal events likely contributing to metastatic progression. Homozygous
CDKN2A deletion and MYC amplification reinforce aggressive proliferative
and anti-apoptotic tumor biology.

The high VAFs of KRAS (42.3%), TP53 (39.1%), and APC (44.7%) — all near
or above 40% in a tumor with estimated 55% cellularity — indicate these
are clonal, founder-level events present in essentially all tumor cells.
Lower VAF alterations including SMAD4 (18.2%), PIK3CA (11.4%), FBXW7
(9.8%), TCF7L2 (8.1%), and ARID1A (7.3%) likely represent subclonal
events, potentially reflecting intratumoral heterogeneity or selective
pressure during metastatic dissemination.

The SMAD4 p.R361H mutation (VAF 18.2%) is particularly noteworthy in
the context of synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, consistent with
the established role of SMAD4 loss in promoting hematogenous spread in
colorectal carcinoma.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  TIER I — Standard of Care (Therapeutic/Predictive):
    • KRAS p.G12D MUTATION: Precludes cetux (Erbitux) and pani
      (Vectibix). Anti-EGFR therapy is contraindicated per NCCN guidelines
      and ASCO recommendations for any RAS-mutant CRC (exon 2, 3, or 4 of
      KRAS or NRAS). Patient is NOT a candidate for anti-EGFR therapy.
    • MSS / pMMR: Precludes first-line Keytruda (Keytruda) monotherapy
      per FDA approval (MSI-H/dMMR only). Standard cytotoxic chemotherapy
      backbones (FOLFOX, FOLFIRI) with VEGF-targeted agents (Avastin,
      ramu, aflibercept) represent appropriate therapeutic strategies.
    • TMB-Low (6.2 mut/Mb): Does not meet threshold for pembro
      TMB-H indication (≥10 mut/Mb).

  TIER II — Investigational / Clinical Trial Consideration:
    • KRAS p.G12D MUTATION: Selective KRAS G12D inhibitors (MRTX1133 and
      related compounds) and pan-KRAS inhibitors are under active
      investigation. Clinical trials combining KRAS G12D inhibition with
      MEK inhibition, SHP2 inhibition, or EGFR blockade are in Phase I/II.
      Patient should be considered for KRAS G12D-directed clinical trial
      enrollment upon disease progression or trial availability.
    • PIK3CA p.E545K MUTATION: PI3K alpha inhibitor trials in CRC (including
      alpelisib-based combinations) may be relevant for future enrollment.
    • CDKN2A Homozygous Deletion / CCND1 Gain / FBXW7 p.R465C:
      CDK4/6 inhibitor-based trials in CRC may be relevant given convergent
      cell cycle dysregulation.
    • MYC Amplification: MYC-targeting strategies (BRD4 inhibitors, CDK7
      inhibitors, aurora kinase inhibitors) in clinical development.
    • ARID1A p.Q2197*: ARID1A-deficient tumors may be sensitive to EZH2
      inhibition (synthetic lethality); clinical trials ongoing.

  TIER III — No Standard Clinical Implication:
    • TCF7L2 p.E225* (VUS): No therapeutic implication.
    • SMAD4 p.R361H: Prognostic (adverse); does not preclude
      Avastin-based therapy at this time.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL PERFORMANCE
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  DNA Quality:           Adequate (DIN 4.2; >50 ng input DNA achieved)
  RNA Quality:           Adequate (DV200 62%)
  Library Preparation:   Hybrid-capture, paired-end sequencing
  Sequencing:            Illumina NovaSeq 6000
  Bioinformatics:        Somatic SNV/indel calling: MuTect2 with artifact
                         filtering; copy number analysis: FACETS; fusion
                         detection: STAR-Fusion and Arriba (concordant
                         results required); MSI: MSIsensor2; TMB:
                         non-synonymous somatic mutations ≥5 mut/Mb
                         threshold across 1.2 Mb coding region.
  Analytical Sensitivity: SNV/indel: >5% VAF at ≥100x coverage.
  Note: This assay is validated for somatic variant detection in tumor
  samples with ≥20% tumor cellularity. Subclonal variants at low VAF
  (<10%) should be interpreted with caution.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MOLECULAR PATHOLOGY ATTESTATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This report was reviewed and approved by:
[Molecular Pathologist, MD/PhD — Department of Molecular Pathology]
CAP-accredited, CLIA-certified laboratory

This report is intended for use by qualified healthcare professionals
in conjunction with all available clinical, histopathological, and
imaging findings. Genomic findings should be interpreted in a
multidisciplinary context. Clinical trial matching consultation
recommended given the complex molecular profile identified.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
END OF REPORT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
